I did think about this; I spent some time thinking through the implications of disallowing model classes with free typevars from being instantiated at all, and ultimately I think this approach is preferable.

I couldn't think of any cases where an error was merited that mypy wouldn't be able to catch with an appropriate configuration if you wanted to be aggressive about your types (i.e., using generic models with implicit Any parameter). And it felt to me that the flexibility benefits of being able to work with unspecified generics was worth more than other forms of strictness here.

-----

The only pattern that I could imagine being problematic would be something along the following lines:
```python
class MyGenericModel(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    my_field: T

MyConcreteModel = MyGenericModel[MyType]
```
If there was a non-pathological example where `MyGenericModel(my_field=instance_of_my_type).my_field` could end up different than `MyConcreteModel(my_field=instance_of_my_type).my_field` due to (again, non-pathological) validator usage, etc., I think that would be concerning. I couldn't think of anything that would cause this though without going out of our way (e.g., by having non-idempotent validators or something like that).
No, no need, thanks for sharing, I was not aware of this.

Wouldn't this technically change the behavior if the caller of `Invocable` modifies args/kwargs after the fact? For example,
```
func = ... # something random
kwargs = {}
obj = Invocable(func=func, kwargs=kwargs)
kwargs["key1"] = 0
```
In your suggested change, since `{}` is falsey, the `Invocable` would get a new dict as its field, whereas in the current code it would share a reference to the originally passed-in dictionary.
This case is pretty pathological and I don't think we'd have situations where args/kwargs get modified after this constructor is called, but I'd just like to check whether it's reasonable to program defensively here.

This would drop any `behavior` that the original `array` had. That is, if `array.behavior` is not None, then the array that this function returns should have that same `behavior`. It can only be overridden if the user-specified `behavior` is not None.

These rules are codified in utility functions `behavior_of` and `wrap` with the `like` argument. See their [implementation here](https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/blob/982b507094bb568f724991c39c3b528abb8c99eb/src/awkward/_v2/_util.py#L459-L502).

I'm only realizing this now because Coffea tests failed when this was accidentally removed from v1 `ak.unzip`. Here's me putting it back earlier today: #1310. (I explicitly reassigned `behavior`; the same thing could be accomplished in v2 by passing a `like=array` argument to `ak._v2._util.wrap`.) Since we don't test `behavior`-forwarding carefully but Coffea uses it a lot, there may be a lot of v2 functions that are missing this forwarding. I haven't been thinking about it while reviewing these PRs.

Since this is probably an across-the-board problem, let's not worry about it here, but fix it in a sweep across all the `ak._v2.*` functions, all at once. Here's the test that I used for `ak.unzip` (v1):

```python
>>> ak.unzip(ak.Array([{"x": 1}], behavior={}))[0].behavior
{}
```

The `ak.Array` is constructed with a `behavior` that is not equal to None, but is anyway simple (empty dict). The `ak.unzip` is _not_ given a `behavior` argument, so the `behavior` property of the output array must be that empty dict. When the `behavior` is not forwarded, the output is None.

We don't need to test the fancy rules (how an explicit `behavior` overrides the forwarded one) because the most likely mistake is that forwarding was forgotten.

I'm merging this PR because it's fine, apart from the behavior-forwarding, and I'm making a [Project](https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/projects/4) card to check it for all functions.

The `tf.data` prefetching is meant to overlap upstream producers (e.g. data loading, map operations) with downstream consumers (trainers) on each training step. However, we're already doing this kind of prefetching and pipelining at the Datasets level, and in this integration, the Datasets batch iterator is the producer for `tf.data`, so `tf.data` would just be prefetching batches from blocks that are already in CPU memory. Since the Datasets prefetching is meant to be driven by the trainer's demand, I think that adding `tf.data` prefetching would just be a confuser that might add more overhead (background thread + batch buffer) than its worth.

I think that we should still have the string shorthand for the sake of convenience, and for those that are used to the Pandas aggregation API. It's particularly convenient if you're just looking for the default configuration of an aggregation, which should be the common case. The contract between these two components is that the upper component should provide `what` to fetch (which has another contract with the graph-control but it doesn't matter where that comes from in loader's point of view) and graph-loader will do whatever it can to fetch the resources and provide the `outGraph`, `outGraphHierarchy`, and `outStats`. 

With that in mind,
1. Can we pass only subset of the Router instead of implementing all Router interface?
This was the most tempting route but I dissuaded myself by arguing that the graph-dashboard or the graph-app should not be aware of how the URL is formed or which route is used by the loader. As long as, for sake of satisfying odd requirement of the graph-app, the router is passed, the upper component can be oblivious to how the data is being fetched.

Now a counter argument can be that an upper component generally are more business logic dense and should be aware of capability of the loader, thus, should kinda know which API end points will be used. This is fair point but I think both approaches are valid.

2. Can we form URLs in the `selection` or somewhere and pass it to the loader like pre-#1853 days?
I think this violates the principle I tried to follow. Upper component should be as ignorant as possible about _how_ the data is fetched as long as it can define _what_ data it wants to fetch.

3. graph-specific Router?
This again makes the upper component aware of capability + implementation details of how the data is loaded.

I do not think it is strictly necessary to block deploying the snap to edge on all tests passing, however, I think it's the behavior I'd ideally like to have as I can think of some (unlikely) scenarios where there are problems affecting the snap not caught by our tests on it. Maybe we found an incompatibility between Certbot and boulder. Maybe our unit tests or mypy found a problem in a code path not tested by the integration tests run on the snap. This is possible even if these tests are run on PRs because we do not require PRs to be up-to-date with `master` before being merged. Basically, if we've found unexpected problems in our code and we're unsure if they affect the snap, why deploy it?

The main downside I can think of in blocking deployments in this way limiting the discussion to the state of things in this PR is our full test suite [takes ~8 minutes longer](https://travis-ci.org/github/bmw/letsencrypt/jobs/678394225) because it's not run in parallel. I don't think this really matters at all for nightly or release builds, but it is kind of annoying for `test-` branches.

(The way things should ideally work in my mind is everything is test and built in parallel with deployments only happening on nightly/release builds after everything else has passed. This requires not only having better support for pipelines/stages than Travis has, but everything being in the same CI provider (or in different CI providers with very good integration with each other).)

Stepping back from that though, again my goal here was simply to merge Robie's code into this repo. I mainly used Travis because Robie used Travis so it made the migration slightly easier. I think almost all of your concerns about Travis are about interactions with future work built on this and are not caused by this PR alone. As far as I can tell, what we have here works just fine with no significant downsides, takes the code from Robie with minimal changes, and sets up snap builds nightly and for future releases.

If that's correct, I'd still personally like to move forward with this PR and save potential future problems for potential future work. If you want, I can remove stages entirely with this PR which eliminates the extra 8 minutes spent testing at the cost of potentially breaking things for people caused by problems caught by our other tests. I'm also happy to add a comment explaining what `SNAP` does when we set it for the snap job.

I can see a future where we pass additional data to this helper function, e.g. environment variables ?

```python
def run_in_subprocess(func_or_options):
    if inspect.isfunction(func_or_options) or inspect.isclass(func_or_options):
        setattr(func_or_options, SUBPROC_TEST_ATTR, True)
        return func_or_options

    options = func_or_options
    def wrapper(obj):
        setattr(obj, SUBPROC_TEST_ATTR, True)
        setattr(obj, SUBPROC_ENV_ATTR, options.get('env'))
        return obj
    return wrapper
```


Then can use as:

```python
@run_in_subprocess(dict(env=dict(DD_TRACE_DEBUG='true')))
def test_dd_trace_debug(self):
    assert os.environ.get('DD_TRACE_DEBUG', 'true')
```

```
import time
import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np
import os
tmp = mx.np.random.normal(-1, 1, (64,300000), ctx=mx.gpu())
tic = time.time()
for i in range(20):
   if (i == 5):
        begin = time.time();
   elif (i == 15):
        end = time.time();
   tic = time.time()
   #out = mx.np.argmax(tmp, axis=1)
   out = mx.npx.topk(tmp, k=1, ret_typ='indices', axis=1, dtype=np.int32)
   out.wait_to_read()
   toc = time.time() - tic
   print ("used time %f:"%toc)
avg = (end - begin) / 10
print ("avg time %f"%avg)
```

APS is a little different.  We have a central NFS share for a common set of Bluesky packages, allowing any user to use the software right away.  (We have other Python installations provided this same way.)  The shared installation is updated in the APS downtime between operating cycles (3x/year).

For production use, we recommend a custom conda environment for each instrument so they have full control of the software suite.  The environment is saved in version control with the other configuration (devices, plans, utils, ...).  We recommend GitHub.  If there are privacy concerns, then APS has an internal GitLab server to be used.

Lately, `conda` is taking very long times to solve the package requirements.  We will start to use `micromamba` to create environments and see how that is received.

We tend to avoid relying on PYTHONPATH since many beam lines use Python in other ways.  PYTHONPATH has proven to generate conflicts.  Instead, we rely on activating an environment to get all the paths correct.  For custom user libraries, we show users how to use `sys.path.append()`.

`T5Encoder` inherits from [pytorch's TransformerEncoder](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/5953fd9133c0bdcc0158acf1472fac403bc5f636/torch/nn/modules/transformer.py#L165). The initialization is mostly the same, except that we only compute relative attention bias in the first layer, and the resulting tensor is passed up to the higher layers to avoid re-computing. Lines `732-735` incorporate this distinction. The forward method is a trimmed down version of the pytorch implementation's, which included torchscript optimizations ([lines 206-232](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/5953fd9133c0bdcc0158acf1472fac403bc5f636/torch/nn/modules/transformer.py#L206)) that I've omitted for now. They've also implemented something called [convert_to_nested](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/5953fd9133c0bdcc0158acf1472fac403bc5f636/torch/nn/modules/transformer.py#L235) which I'm not too sure of its purpose.

So, `get_comma_header` and `set_comma_header` both expect lowercase headers.

We've got two options with this PR:

* Switch both `get_comma_header` and `set_comma_header` to expect case-sensitive headers, and lowercase them for the lookup behaviour. Preserve whatever header casing is used when calling `set_comma_header`.
* Continue to expect lowercase headers to both `get_comma_header` and `set_comma_header`. When setting up the header casing in `set_comma_header`, use title casing.

Of those two, I've gone for the second, as being marginally less impactful, and also being marginally quicker. (Only requires a casing transform in one of the two cases.)

Both of these two are private methods, so we're okay for them to have behaviour that only makes sense if they're being used in an appropriate context. In particular, the docstring for `get_comma_header` already lists the full set of header cases that it's actually being used with.

I've now added a corresponding docstring to `set_comma_header`, which I think helps clarify (internally), why the title casing is sufficient and appropriate for this function.

1. **Compressed + Parquet-encoded, on disk:** In addition to Parquet's native column encoding (dictionary, RLE, delta), the file is also compressed with e.g. Snappy or gzip.
2. **Parquet-encoded, on disk:** Just Parquet's native column encoding (dictionary, RLE, delta), no other compression.
3. **Parquet-encoded, in memory:** Just Parquet's native column encoding (dictionary, RLE, delta), no other compression.
4. **Arrow format, in memory:** Fully decompressed, including both Parquet's column encoding and any other compression, represented in Arrow's in-memory format.

It's my understanding that:
- Arrow is giving us (2),
- (2) and (3) are equivalent in terms of size in bytes,
- we don't read the full Parquet-encoded file into memory and then decode, we instead read in chunks of row groups that progressively decoded.

For those reasons, I was thinking that referring to this multiplier/ratio as being on top of "Parquet's on-disk representation" is more accurate, but I might be misunderstanding some aspect of this.

`type_ids` doesn't have to come from the indexer. In my model, it comes from the dataset reader. That model uses `type_ids` to distinguish the context (which may contain the answer) from question and special tokens (which may not).

I could buy the argument that then the reader should not produce these, but it makes the code much more convenient if I can handle it in one place. Most of the time, `type_ids` are just a "bonus" feature that the model may or may not take advantage of, and RoBERTa does not.

For some models though, `type_ids` carry critical information. In those cases, it would be an error to use RoBERTa at all. I've built such a model before. To get it to work, I trained the type embeddings from scratch during fine-tuning (as they are discussing here: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/issues/1234). It works, and beats the other transformers on the task. It would be a neat feature if we could offer RoBERTa with type ids, but that's out of scope for this PR.

Yes this is different, `NumberT` is only a `Union` of types, this `NumT` gets bound to one of the types in the signature of a function. Small example:
```python
import typing as t

NumberT = t.Union[int, float]
NumT = t.TypeVar("NumT", int, float)

def add(x: NumberT, y: NumberT) -> NumberT:
    return x + y

def add_generic(x: NumT, y: NumT) -> NumT:
    return x + y
    
reveal_type(add(5, 5))  # Revealed type is "Union[builtins.int, builtins.float]"
reveal_type(add_generic(5, 5))  # Revealed type is "builtins.int"
```
So here `NumberT` is always fixed to a union of types. But `NumT` gets bound to the detected type of `x` and `y` hence the return type is also `int`.

I believe that [`Formatters.format`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/9af0e47b1705457bb6b327c197f2ec5737a1d8f6/Lib/logging/__init__.py#L651-L681) is the intended place to do this kind of string formatting. Which is also not exactly duplicate, we binpack internally on all nodes (pending/launching/running) but outside we binpack on (pending/running), the logic differs between terminating and launching. When launching we are very conservative in adding nodes, when terminating we are very conservative in terminating nodes (opposite of conservative in adding). If a node is launching in upscaling, we count it, if it is launching when we terminate we don't count it (potentially keeping non-pending nodes for longer time).

This just reminded me I have to make a fix here. There is no guarantee that exceptions will be pickle-able, and so we can't send an exception from a worker process back to the main process. So instead, we just send the traceback as a string and a string representation of the exception back to the main process. So the best we can is print out the traceback to stderr from the main process, since the traceback that the main process creates will not include the traceback from the worker.

I don't think we can use blocking puts and gets and end up with a solution that is robust to deadlocks. Let's see if this works:
- Producer ended up filling the queue and waits on a blocking put.
- Consumer fails. Calls `iterator.stop.set()`, however since .put is blocking within an iterator queue, the event is never checked and the thread is not shut down.

Another scenario: 
- The queue is empty, hence consumer waits on a blocking `.get`. 
- However, producer raises an exception. The thread dies and the consumer is stuck forever on a .get.

There's 9 mutable variables needed just for fold. This is fine in this draft PR, to demonstrate that `@fold` works. However, this is where most of the code complexity comes from, and can lead to bugs. Mutable state is the hardest to document, so let's have as little of it as possible. I think simplifying the state is a worthwhile investment, and it looks to me like it would be necessary to implement traversals inside fold. Some techniques you can try:
- Try to adopt the existing fields instead of defining new ones when possible. Is it necessary to store both `_current_location` and `_fold_location`? Do you have to update both of them when you're inside fold? Is it necessary to have both `_aliases` and `_folded_output_aliases`?
- Group some fields into a namedtuple, reducing visual clutter. Examples:
    - The query components like `_fold_from_clause`, `_fold_outputs` and `_fold_group_by` are all part of one query. You could create a `QueryBuilder` class or namedtuple that contains all parts of the query, which will simplify even the non-fold code that generates the final result.
    - The `_fold_count` and `_folded_output_count` are only used to generate unique names, but take up a central position in the complicated state we need to manage. You can pull out this functionality out of this class into a `UniqueAliasGenerator`, making these things invisible.
- Derive some of the fields from others, converting them into pure functions. See `self._current_classname` as an example.

There might be other ways that I'm not seeing. Feel free to play around and see what works.

2. Ideally we only need to put non-target-roots into `'transitive_targets`. Also relating to Borja's work to minimize the # of intellij modules. Do you think below whether below makes sense, @blorente?
For example:
```
Given
A -> B -> C -> D
A -> E -> F

$ ./pants export-dep-as-jar A
{
 A: {'transitive_targets', [B, C, D, E, F], is_target_root: True}
 B: // dependencies here doesn't matter because it's not target root, only need to show where the jar is
 C: // same
 D: // same
 E: // same
 F: // same
}

$ ./pants export-dep-as-jar A B
{ 
  A: { targets: [B], transitive_targets: [E,F], is_target_root: True}, // because B is also a target root
  B: { transitive_targets: [C, D], is_target_root: True}
  C: // dependencies here doesn't matter because it's not target root, only need to show where the jar is
  D: // same
  E: // same
  F: // same
```

Although the tricky part could be
```
A -> B -> C -> D
./pants export-dep-as-jar A C
```
In this case, we might have to force B into target root (intellij module rather than a library), because "3rdparty library" (B in this case from A's perspective) in intellij cannot depend back onto C.

        triggerable = check_supports(obj, Triggerable)
        triggerable.trigger()
        readable = check_supports(obj, Readable)

The problem is that mypy won't allow multiple type narrowing with isinstance in a single function, so you couldn't do:

assert isinstance(obj, Triggerable)

```

While I never used Semantic Release, two things come into my mind:
* Decision to release should be left to humans, we do not want too many releases, and we didn't even add AI  ;)
* Reading https://github.com/semantic-release/semantic-release#commit-message-format -- i do not think that commit messages are safe to use for release notes, main reason is that once merged you cannot change it. It is too easy to merge wrong stuff. That is why almost everyone is using an other ways of dealing with release notes either using fragments, static changelog or fully outside git.

Altering commit message does retrigger the whole testing pipelines. That is why I prefer the idea of dealing with release notes on a OOB channel, as it is mostly bureaucracy, needed but it should not block or slowdown the PRs.

Please note that I did not propose to ditch current changelog (or the guidelines for changes). So mainly what we do now is to experiment how good is this approach for our project. I do expect to copy/paste the release notes and merge them to the changelog before each release, at least until we are confident that it works fine. I do not want to confuse existing users with something the cores are only exploring.

        from google.auth.credentials import AnonymousCredentials
        from google.cloud.storage._helpers import STORAGE_EMULATOR_ENV_VAR
        
        HOST = "https://api.example.com"
        PROJECT = "test"

> Can you elaborate on that? Doesn't `tqdm` just copy a tiny tensor, instead of all the parameters?

Yes. When the GPU operations are executed with the current data batch (supposing GPU usage), they live in GPU memory and never do GPU-CPU sync, and are executed async actually because they're enqueued by PyTorch transparently. When any GPU variable is read and needs to be used in CPU memory, the CUDA device is gonna be synchronized (waiting for the necessary operations to finish execution) and transferred to CPU memory, to then continue with the execution. This happens when displaying the metrics with tqdm:

1. The batch is executed on GPU. The metrics are computed on GPU.
2. The value is copied to CPU to show in tqdm.
3. Next batch...

The next batch waits for the transfer in the middle. Which is different from not waiting for GPU-CPU transfers (which are expensive relative to GPU execution) between many batch executions. It could be solved with some `log_every` option or similar.

Btw, it'd be good to measure this somehow (e.g, show there are no transfers with some profiling, doing transfers on purpose to see how slow it is).

As a side note, I'm not 100% confident this is how it works, but I'm sure enough to raise the concern.

Relevant documentation: [Asynchronous execution - CUDA Semantics - PyTorch master documentation](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/cuda.html#asynchronous-execution).

@stephanwlee After some further thinking, I decided that the current approach is reasonable and more appropriate compared to the two options you mentioned. The reason is as follows:

1. The human-readable string names of the dtypes (e.g., "float16") are not a part of the protobuf type definition it self (which is at https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/framework/types.proto#L12). The string names of the proto types (e.g., "DT_FLOAT") is not the most human-friendly to show. Instead, the human-readable strings are defined at the Python level, at https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/master/tensorboard/compat/tensorflow_stub/dtypes.py#L480.

As such, it is natural to populate the map from Python. Doing so also leaves open the possibility of attaching additional properties of the types in the response in the future, e.g., including fields such as "is_floating", "is_integer", and "is_string", which are also not pieces of information directly available from the protobuf definition.

This is argument against option 2 above.

2. With regard to option 1 (encoding the semantics, e.g., "dtype" and "num_nans") in the response itself. This will cause the response format to deviate from the on-disk format for the execution data and therefore increase the complexity of the backend. Increase in the size of the response is another minor concern, as mentioned above. 

Based on these considerations, I'd like to stick with the current approach. In the meantime, I've slightly optimized the Python code in `debugger_v2_plugin.py`, so that the `dtypes_map` is populated once, when the plugin is created, instead of being created on the fly during every request.

I like both `AssetNode` and `AssetInfo` but for pretty different reasons:

`AssetNode` feels like a self-contained/singular concept, whereas `AssetInfo` implies to me that there are two things: assets and info, and `AssetInfo` is some info/metadata about an existing asset object. 

And i think that could have some effects on docs/mental model etc. I'm thinking about it like this:
* With `AssetNode` there could be more conflation of the software object and the persistent data in storage. Like the `AssetNode` *is* the data asset. In our docs we will often just say `asset` without clarifying if we're talking about the persistent data asset or the software-defined asset, and that works in a world view where we think of them as kind of the same thing. i think `AssetNode` would fit into that prose and mental model more seamlessly
* With `AssetInfo` the implication of two concepts makes `AssetInfo` seem more like a pointer to the persistent data in storage. There's a bit more separation between the two and we could lean into the "a software-defined asset is a software *representation* of a data asset" mindset. That could manifest in the docs with us being more specific about whether we are talking about the persistent data asset or the software-defined asset. I'm not sure if that would be a good or bad thing though

And unfortunately, there's not a real short answer to what 'total' memory means.  We're wrapping the cuda call cudaMemGetInfo(), and the NVIDIA documentation says:
```
Returns in *free and *total respectively, the free and total amount of memory available for allocation by the device in bytes.
```
Let's say you've got a GPU with published memory T.  The GPU driver puts some control structures like the page table in that memory, so call that driver overhead D.  Finally, your GPU may be driving a monitor, so a window manager is using the GPU with overhead W.  So what does the API return for 'total' in this scenario?  The answer is T - D.  The long answer then is: 'total' means the total memory available to both your MXNet process **and** other processes that may be using the GPU.  I don't know a way to suggest this succinctly without introducing more confusion.

> The util case linked above is not of the style Yi pointed out, but rather a hack with ~aggregator targets to support a legacy layout / jar contents. 

As you said: this is very useful for legacy targets.

When you split a jar from one jar into many jars, it is important for the original jar/pom to not suddenly cease to exist, for a few reasons:

1. If the jar/pom ceases to exist, then you break consumers that are depending on nightly snapshots of a monorepo, without a deprecation cycle.
2. If the consumers do not have a direct dependency on the old jar, but do have a transitive dependency, you can get in a situation where one subgraph depends on the old merged jar, while another subgraph depends on the split jars. Because there is no shared artifact name between these subgraphs, ivy/maven/coursier will not resolve them for you... if you preserve the legacy jar/pom for a deprecation period, you minimize the chance of this.

----

We would be fine with automagically converting something to a `<packaging>pom</packaging>` artifact if it has no sources, but "guaranteeing a jar even if it is empty" seemed like it involved fewer special cases.

For clients, we'll need to get a new section added to the bottom of the [guide](https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/develop/docs/source/guide/clients.rst), similar to Resource and Session.

Feel free to riff on this if you feel there's better phrasing or layout, but something along these lines would be a great start:

## Multithreading or multiprocessing with Clients
Unlike Resources and Sessions, clients **are** generally _thread-safe_. There are some caveats, defined below, to be aware of though.

### Caveats

**Multi-Processing:** While clients are _thread-safe_, they cannot be shared across processes due to their networking implementation. Doing so may lead to incorrect response ordering when calling services.

**Shared Metadata:** Clients expose metadata to the end user through a few attributes (namely `meta`, `exceptions` and `waiter_names`). These are safe to read but any mutations should not be considered thread-safe.

**Custom [Botocore Events](https://botocore.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/topics/events.html):** Botocore (the library Boto3 is built on) allows advanced users to provide their own custom event hooks which may interact with boto3's client. The majority of users will not need to use these interfaces, but those that do should no longer consider their clients thread-safe without careful review.

### General Example

```python
import boto3.session
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

def do_s3_task(client, task_definition):
    # Put your thread-safe code here

def my_workflow():
    # Create a session and use it to make our client
    session = boto3.session.Session()
    s3_client = session.client('s3')

    # Define some work to be done, this can be anything
    my_tasks = [ ... ]

    # Dispatch work tasks with our s3_client
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8) as executor:
        futures = [executor.submit(do_s3_task, s3_client, task) for task in my_tasks]
```

I think the reason I didn't combine them was two-fold: one, `ModelField` had an attribute called `final` in v1, but not `frozen`. So it felt a little weird to remove `final`, especially considering conceptually it's different from `frozen` (for starters, in theory we shouldn't allow overriding the default, though I suspect it's not worth the effort to enforce outside of type checking).

Second, I imagined we might eventually want to give different error messages depending on frozen vs. final. (I could imagine a newer user getting an error message saying 'frozen' when you just annotated as Final and possibly don't even know what frozen refers to could be confusing.) In which case perhaps it would be helpful to have the distinction.

However, in this PR, I just set the frozen attribute on the field based on `frozen=field_info.frozen or field_info.final`. So yeah maybe we should just combine the two and only store `frozen` on the field.

If you read the above and don't have an opinion/haven't changed your mind, I suppose I'll just combine them and drop the `final` attribute, and it can be a feature request in the future.

> I don't think a gallery example (do you mean this by narrative documentation) is a good replacement for a well written short summary or notes section in the docstring.

I don't think either! Btw, yes, by narrative documentation I mean the tutorial-like gallery examples. They are not supposed to be a *replacement* for a docstring; we are dealing with *different levels* here (I mean 'levels' as in high-level vs. low-level, general-purpose vs. domain-specific, ...).

> Gallery examples are often not directly focused on the function itself.

Exactly, here you go. That's why, when writing the docstring, we shouldn't assume a *very particular use* of the function itself. The second sentence in the abstract of the paper you were pointing to goes like: "Our clinical studies indicate that they are more discrete in younger and in more heavily pigmented individuals, ..." This is super specific (e.g., clinical trials, comparison between younger and older individuals, ...) and completely unrelated to the image dataset we are sharing...

> Especially if it is distributed among X different gallery examples.

Exactly, again. The docstring of the function itself is not the right place to discuss a) very very specific applications of the function nor (worse) b) applications which are not even related to the actual function (here, data). As far as we know, the dataset at hand isn't part of the research presented in that paper.

> It probably depends on personal preference

Well, I would argue that there is a rationale. At first, I thought that pointing to that paper would be somewhat equivalent to linking to the 'astronaut' entry in the dictionary for the `data.astronaut()` function. So I thought 'why not?' (replying rather no, with the argument that it's pretty much unnecessary; see above).

Then I realized it was even worse (logically speaking), because at least the 'astronaut' entry in the dictionary is *generic* so, even if unnecessary, it's not properly off-topic. But this paper investigates the potential role of the palisades of Vogt in the aging/diseases of the cornea... (??)

Btw, the current (WIP) gallery example which uses this image of 'palisades of Vogt' is about dark spots caused by dust on the microscope's mirror (!!) -- which proves that, indeed and as you have pointed out yourself, what's found in a function's docstring should have nothing to do (*a priori*) with the introductory section of a given tutorial/paper.

> what precisely we understand as "context & motivation"

Sure, there can be different levels of "context & motivation;" in a scientific setting,  by "context & motivation" I mean basically the first section of a research paper.

I think we should generally discuss whether we want to go with attribute-based `fetch_state` implementations, or with `PhysicalResourceId` based `fetch_state` implementations.

Both have their pros and cons, but I would generally try to be more lenient when it comes to deploying stacks into environments where certain resources already exist.

In other words: The previous implementation allowed CF stacks to be deployed in a LocalStack instance where a VPC with a particular `CidrBlock` already exists. With the new implementation (after your changes), the CF stack creation will likely fail if such a VPC already exists (because `self.physical_resource_id` is None, and when CF tries to run `create_vpc(..)` it will fail because a VPC with this `CidrBlock` already exists).

From a usability point of view, I think we should rather be more lenient (less strict) and try use the **attribute-based `fetch_state` implementation** (whenever possible). I can, for example, anticipate issues where we may have different support levels for persistence - e.g., if certain resources are already deployed because they have persistence enabled, and then CF deployments would fail.

---
To **summarize** the points above: I think we should revert these changes and leave the original implementation, to avoid users running into weird issues in different use cases. Thanks

IOW, note that `pex -o pants.pex pantsbuild-Pants` installs `pantsbuild.pants`, yet 

```
>>> from pkg_resources import Requirement
>>> Requirement.parse("pantsbuild.pants") == Requirement.parse("pantsbuild-Pants")
False
```

So that `__eq__` implementation is not actually correct for how we're using it. And it introduces the bug of not handling `:all:`. Plus, these strings are not full requirement strings but just project names, so parsing them as requirements and using that object's `__eq__` seems like overkill.

So, I think the simple solution is to ~revert those parts of this change, and keep these as normalized strs.

Also note that the problem this PR exists to fix is pretty minor in practice - it seems unlikely that users will pick arbitrary variants of the same package names in `only_binary` and `no_binary`, and if they do, some extra invalidation doesn't seem terrible.

In general, we should avoid using `string.format` for logging. 
Because when using `string.format`, the message string always needs to be formatted, even when the actual logging level is higher than `info`.
Instead, we should use `logger.info("message %s %s", arg1, arg2)`, which have little overhead when logging level is higher than `info`.
This problem exists in a lot of places in Ray code base. We should avoid it in the future.

The more I think about supporting multiple exporters, the better it sounds to have one queue per worker. If we did this the tracer could add spans to a wrapper that adds a span to the queue for each registered exporter. This is what I assume you mean by aggregated exporter, and it's what the agent does in [multiconsumer.go](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-service/blob/master/processor/multiconsumer/multiconsumer.go).

If the aggregated exporter has a single underlying queue we can only drain the queue as fast as the longest export interval (/smallest MTU), which could cause us to drop spans that other exporters would otherwise export in time. It also makes the implementation more complex.

For now all we need to do is make sure the design in this PR doesn't preclude multiple exporters, we don't actually have to support them yet.

"Starting" was chosen to denote that it was the sequence number applied to the first event in the batch.   The batch itself is not assigned a sequence number, nor is it considered a single item by the service.   

To determine the next sequence number that the service expects,  one has to take the batch's starting sequence number and increment it by the number of events that were in the batch.   This caused confusion for our beta users that expected the "LastPublishedSequenceNumber" returned as part of the partition publishing properties did not align with the "PublishedSequenceNumber" that we assigned to the batch.   It was agreed that the prefix "Starting" helped as a mnemonic.

> 1. What's the difference between `operator itype` and `operator itype&&`? Both look the same.

Both variants are needed to actually trigger a move construction in both of the following cases:
- for a function `foo(T&&)`, explicitly trigger a move construction via `operator itype`
  Just passing the rvalue reference, wouldn't trigger a move construction!
- for a function `foo(T)` with a non-copy-constructible type, implicitly trigger a move construction
  via `operator itype&&`

Introducing the extra cast operator is just a technical trick to actually achieve the correct move-construction behavior.
I also tried [including this logic into `cast_op<T>()`](https://github.com/rhaschke/pybind11/blob/36ba36e892aeac3adb48e59d5edc313be2521713/include/pybind11/cast.h#L976-L981) instead. While this approach will remove the need to define the redundant `operator itype()`, my naive implementation returns a reference to a local temporary. Alternatively changing the return type based on the two cases, will make `caster::cast_op_type` inconsistent to what `cast_op` actually returns. Maybe, you, @bstaletic, have a better idea to implement it there.

> 2. If we want `f(T)` to be a copy (which seems reasonable), why is `operator itype` using `std::move()`?

In C++, the `f(T)` uses a copy constructor for lvalue references and a move constructor for rvalue references.
Returning `std::move()` from `operator itype()` essentially yields `itype(std::move(arg))`, which realizes the move construction.

> 3. Should the operators be ref-qualified, as in `operator itype&&() &&`?

I think this extra rvalue-qualifier was used in the past to restrict the move operation to situations where the caster itself is an rvalue. However, due to [`cast_op<T>(caster&&)`](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/2047/files#diff-b8e97eea5f84a84248db1bc72afaccfd8ba28cad023917d52bdc6bbc3669cd74L953-L957), the cast_op_type is always an rvalue reference if the caster is an rvalue reference. There is no need to require an rvalue caster also for the actual casting operation.

> Did you consider just having `Value` be an `interface{}` and be either a `string` or `map`?

So, right now `Value` is always going to be a valid value. If `Object` is true, it'll be a JSON encoded string.

I didn't want to make `Value` be an actual object when `Object` is true, as that could break existing users who may be using this programmatically and always expecting Value to be a string.

The concept of "encoding" means how certain abstract characters are stored at byte level. As such, it makes sense to talk about the encoding of a file (because a file is just a sequence of bytes), or the encoding of a `bytes` object, but there is no such thing as "encoding" of a python string (a python string has an underlying encoding, of course, but it's complicated, and mutable, and invisible to the user).

In short, when input is a string the concept of encoding does not apply. Or at least shouldn't. In your example it will actually garble the input, which is a bug.

This change will get the edge cause. Notice the new `CompilerPanic` (which you may want to handle separately by just not applying the operation and returning the un-clamped `exp` operation)
```suggestion
    a_is_negative = a < 0
    a = abs(a)  # No longer need to know if it's signed or not
    if a == 0 or a == 1:
        raise CompilerPanic("Exponential operation is useless!")
        
    # NOTE: There is an edge case if `a` were left signed where the following
    #       operation would not work (`ln(a)` is undefined if `a <= 0`)
    b = int(Decimal(value_bits) / (Decimal(a).ln() / Decimal(2).ln()))
    if b <= 1:
        return 1  # Value is assumed to be in range, therefore power of 1 is max

    # Do a bit of iteration to ensure we have the exact number
    num_iterations = 0
    while a ** (b + 1) < 2 ** value_bits:
        b += 1
        num_iterations += 1
        assert num_iterations < 10000
    while a ** b >= 2 ** value_bits:
        b -= 1
        num_iterations += 1
        assert num_iterations < 10000

    # Edge case: If a is negative and the values of a and b are such that:
    #               (a) ** (b + 1) == -(2 ** value_bits)
    #            we can actually squeak one more out of it because it's on the edge
    if a_is_negative and (-a) ** (b + 1) == -(2 ** value_bits):  # NOTE: a = abs(a)
        return b + 1
    else:
        return b  # Exact
```

Yes, that's right. Currently if the student has incorrect syntax then `execute` is where the error will be thrown. But if we decide to let SQLAlchemy and the dbapis handle the automatic starting of transactions (as in the code excerpt above), then that means we will also be preventing the execution of `BEGIN|START|COMMIT`. So any invalid syntax _won't_ be raised there. From a pedagogical perspective I think there may be a point to just executing the user's queries, period, if only to allow the user to learn from mistakes in syntax. And if it means we execute the user's `COMMIT` and then also call the method `commit` (so as to clear the database state as well) then it may be worth the educational benefit, even though we end up committing an empty transaction.

Raises the question from the PR description about how to model the Helm chart as targets. For example, does Pants actually need to track the template files individually?

For some language backends, the answer is yes; for example, in the JVM (Java/Scala), each source file is tracked individually so Pants can only compile the subset that actually depend on each other. Whereas the Go backend compiles by Go package, so `go_package` tracks all Go files in a package.

It seems fine to me to start with Helm support without these additional target types. If they turn out to be necessary in the future, they can be introduced then.

(As an aside, I recall that rules can also just implement `GenerateTargetsRequest` directly without using `TargetGenerator`. See `GoModTarget` in the Go backend which does not subclass `TargetGenerator`.)

Stepping back up from the weeds of this PR: If you're a Pants user and you're using Pants to do `<vertical>` for you, then if that vertical does not have tools Pants uses that embed their own certs and those tools are run via Pants in a container with standard env vars not set or not plumbed through Pants, you'll have this same issue all over again in that other vertical. So you get a picture of ~O(10) verticals each with their own embdded root certs of varying age and content (for those that do embed). It seems like the only sane thing is to have a single root cert set and plumb that through to all tools. You can then keep that cert set up2date like you do the rest of the packages on your machine.

So, yeah, its convenient Pip and now Pex will be magically "saving you from a headache" - but you'll just be avoiding the truth. And a headache will turn into a bigger problem further down the line.

🤔 Some thinking out loud alternatives:

1) (Current) Set a "protocol" tag on every span and have special cases in a generic Prometheus observer.

Weird dispatcher in the Prom observer, metric classes need to know about client/server implementation details like what tags are there and what they mean.

2) (This) Ditch the dispatching Prometheus observer and instead have Prom observer classes specific to a client or server (or subset of those). 

This acknowledges the tight coupling between client/server details and prometheus, and gets rid of the dispatcher. 

Would we want to keep the de facto interface we currently have on metric classes of `active_requests_metric`, `latency_seconds_metric` and so on? Are we sure it always makes sense, looking at the current things we have in Wavefront?

We'll probably still end up with lots of classes that are very similar except for some details like what tags to look at, or latency distribution. Instead of passing a metric class here we could pass an instance of a class, and then potentially change things like the distribution buckets from the client/server class without making a new metric class.

```
span.set_metrics_cls(PrometheusSqlClientMetrics(latency_buckets={...}))
```

3) Take it a step further and not use the observer pattern, and just build prom into the client/server classes.

Easy, less code, can target what we actually need. Introduces a specific dependency on prom being imported (even if it does nothing when prom is not enabled) which some people don't like? Spans outside baseplate would need to implement prom themselves (good?!).

4) Keep the dispatching Prometheus observer, remove the protocol tag and instead inspect the call stack to see where the span is from.

Keeps the span/observer abstraction clean, not needing to change every client and server. Still reliant on the prom classes knowing implementation details of the clients, probably a bit too magic.

The problem is a bit complicated to describe, so here is the short version:
the parameters you are using here come from the settings object given to the `extract_features` function. Due to reference/pointer magic happening in python, the `kwargs` you are using here is actually the *exact* one stored in the settings object. If you now use these setings twice in the same test (which all of those failed tests do), you actually *remove* `features` from the original settings object and will not be present the next time :-)
So, simple fix: `add kwargs = kwargs.copy()` before that.

I believe, based on https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4972#discussion_r838027789, that `{parallel}` should be removed from this PR, but in case this comes up in the future I'll explain here the evidence in favor of putting it in `Imports`.

"Writing R Extensions", in the ["Package Dependencies"](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html#Package-Dependencies) sub-section in the section about `DESCRIPTION`, explicitly states that package authors should include "all the standard packages that are used".

> The ‘Imports’ field lists packages whose namespaces are imported from (as specified in the NAMESPACE file) but which do not need to be attached... Ideally this field will include all the standard packages that are used

The only package which it says not to include is `{base}`.

> It makes no sense to declare a dependence on R without a version specification, nor on the package **base**: this is an R package and package **base** is always available

As shown in the link above, `{lightgbm}` includes other default packages like `{graphics}` and `{utils}` in `Imports:`. Those have been there since the first CRAN release in October 2020, and CRAN has never complained about them.

The CRAN mirror on GitHub shows many packages on CRAN which list `{parallel}` in their `Imports`: https://github.com/search?p=2&q=org%3Acran+filename%3ADESCRIPTION+%27parallel%2C%27&type=Code.

Including packages written by R-core members, like `{iotools}` ([link](https://github.com/cran/iotools/blob/714368d3f0785d5e3e6b16d174c80cc4cc57be70/DESCRIPTION#L7)).

I proposed in SPEC-1025 to have the Child Update Request always be treated as a peer MLE message. That would simplify this line to:

    `aRxInfo.mClass = RxInfo::kPeerMessage;`

The behavior would be fully equivalent I think, because if the peer message is processed and the originator ("neighbor") has IsStateValid() == false, then the particular tKSC advancement code for peer messages would not be executed anyway. So then the processing becomes equivalent to tentative class. So it seems not needed to change the class dynamically to "tentative" here in some cases, as the class of "peer" already includes the assumption that if you receive such message from a non-peer i.e. unknown node or invalid node then don't treat it; and for sure don't update your tKSC based on it.

I think this relates to another problem: currently data frames and ACKs use a different way to finally update their CSL IE. In `otPlatRadioTxAckStarted`, CSL IE is updated there while in `otPlatRadioTxStarted`, it's not. I think both data frames and ACKs should use the same way to add CSL IE. I remember that there was a discussion about where should the final CSL IE content be generated, in radio layer or in submac layer? I think we should reach an agreement on this. 

In current implementation, for data frames, `CslSampleTime` and `CslPeriod` would be both synchronized to platform radio layer. And hence when it finally updates CSL IE, the radio layer could use a method `getCslPhase` and calcaulate the value itself. For ACK frames, the CSL IE is updated in `otPlatRadioTxAckStarted`, which calls radio callbacks and finally get the value in `SubMac` layer. I think we should unify this.

However, there's another reason for the difference between `otPlatRadioTxAckStarted`  and `otPlatRadioTxStarted`. In https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/examples/platforms/nrf528xx/src/radio.c#L397, `otPlatRadioTxStarted` is called in `otPlatRadioTransmit` instead of an interrupt context(`nrf_802154_tx_started`). And the main purpose of `otPlatRadioTxStarted` is to start a timer. Meanwhile, `otPlatRadioTxAckStarted` is called in `nrf_802154_tx_ack_started`. In summary, the two methods have a different semantic(meaning) for now. So I'm also usually confused by this difference. I think we should make them the same.

As far as I'm concerned, I think we can let radio layer to generate CSL IE itself and we let `otPlatRadioTxAckStarted` have the same meaning with `otPlatRadioTxStarted`. That means, `otPlatRadioTxAckStarted` is not meaningful for us anymore because we use auto ack now, unlike the way to send a data frame. And we would use other method like `otMacFrameSetCslIe` to update its CSL both for data frames and ACKs, and the same for security stuff updates.

I think this should work because `zip` stops when the shortest of the arguments is exhausted. So it is only checking if _all_ the path components are equivalent in the case that the length of the `parts` are equal. But, when one path has more parts than the other, then it is checking that the longer path starts with the shorter path.

Example:
```python3
def startswith(a, b): return all(i == j for i, j in zip(a, b))

a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2]
assert startswith(a, b)
```

Observations, I have to defer to @wjakob  judgement for what to do (if anything):

Here the compiler has to work very hard just to put together the array of char pointers:
```static const char* formats[] = {"?", "b", "B", "h", "H", "i", "I", "q", "Q", "f", "d", "g"};```
That's basically the same as the hard-coded string in `detail/common.h`:
```static constexpr const char c = "?bBhHiIqQfdg"[...];```

It's only a subset of the format strings accepted by the current Python implementation:

https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/e51dd9dad6590bf3a940723fbbaaf4f64a3c9228/Objects/memoryobject.c#L1150

Very unfortunately, this authoritative Python function is hidden away (`static`).
My own choice would be to copy the function into pybind11 (probably with modifications), with a long comment to explain why. I'd also carefully check every released Python version to see if the list evolved over time, to track that in our copy, if necessary.

Alternatively, if we decide we only want the subset of format strings, I'd do a slight refactor of `detail/common.h` to make the list available without the very involved detour through the C++ template engine.

Speedup:
```python
minx = []
maxx = []
miny = []
maxy = []
val = []
for roi in rectangular_rois:
    gravels = self.generate_gravel_patch(roi)
    x = gravels[:, 0]
    y = gravels[:, 1]
    r = random_utils.randint(1, 4, len(gravels))
    r1 = random_utils.randint(0, 255, len(gravels))
    miny.append(np.maximum(y - r, 0))
    maxy.append(np.minimum(y + r, y))
    minx.append(np.maximum(x - r, 0))
    maxx.append(np.minimum(x + r, x))
    val.append(r1)

return {
    "gravels_infos": np.stack([
        np.concatenate(miny),
        np.concatenate(maxy),
        np.concatenate(minx),
        np.concatenate(maxx),
        np.concatenate(val),
    ], 1)
}
```

I have one recommendation for the easier digestion of this tracing. Instead of updating the `_update` method to be traced vs. non-traced Let's have its implementation always be like:

```
def _update(...):
      return self._possible_trace_update(...)


def _untraced_update(...)
        def helper(_batch):
            with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
                fwd_out = self._module.forward_train(_batch)
                loss_per_module = self.compute_loss(fwd_out=fwd_out, batch=_batch)
            gradients = self.compute_gradients(loss_per_module, gradient_tape=tape)
            postprocessed_gradients = self.postprocess_gradients(gradients)
            self.apply_gradients(postprocessed_gradients)

            return fwd_out, loss_per_module, postprocessed_gradients, self._metrics

        return self._strategy.run(helper, args=(batch,))
```

And have `_possible_trace_update()` to get assigned to either `_untraced_update()` or `tf.function(_untraced_update)`. Then the user won't be confused to see `_update` to be a traced vs untraced fn.

Also: `numpy.copyto` assigns the extra array elements when `len(read_array) > len(response.read_array)` but it raises an exception when `len(read_array) < len(response.read_array)`:
```
>>> numpy.copyto(x, [50,51])
>>> x
array([[50, 51],
       [50, 51]])
>>> numpy.copyto(x, [60,61,62,63,64,65])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<__array_function__ internals>", line 200, in copyto
ValueError: could not broadcast input array from shape (6,) into shape (2,2)
```

Out of these possible solutions, I think think `read_array.flat[:len(response.read_array)] = response.read_array` is the best. To ensure that we aren't losing any array elements, we could put it in a helper function with an assertion or runtime check:
```
def assign_numpy_array(numpy_array, grpc_array):
    assert len(grpc_array) >= numpy_array.size:
    numpy_array.flat[:len(grpc_array)] = grpc_array
```

2 issues:

* The interval "1 second" is too small , we need to increase to 10s interval between each worker starting time. Otherwise if many tasks running (e.g. 8 tasks on a node) port conflicts might happen frequently.

* Only spark tasks on a local machine need to set different delay time. e.g., there're 8 tasks, tasks 1-4 in nodeA, other tasks on nodeB, we hope:
task1-4: sleep 0s / 10s / 20s / 30s
task5-8: sleep 0s / 10s / 20s / 30s

To achieve this, because we cannot use `get_spark_task_local_rank` now, we can use exclusive file lock instead:

In each spark task, we first try to acquire an exclusive file lock, and keep locking it for 10s, and then release the file lock.

> Our code style currently says otherwise but I've always disliked that.

I share the feeling. And if one looks at the bytecode difference between `s = {}` and `s = dict()` one sees
```
  2           0 BUILD_MAP                0
              2 STORE_FAST               0 (s)
```
for the first and
```
  2           0 LOAD_GLOBAL              0 (dict)
              2 CALL_FUNCTION            0
              4 STORE_FAST               0 (s)
```
for the second. In other words, using a literal translates to a specific bytecode for dict construction, whereas the other translates to a generic constructor call. Then there's the microbenchmark:
```
[ivan@beta4 ~]$ python -m timeit 's = {}'
20000000 loops, best of 5: 18.9 nsec per loop
[ivan@beta4 ~]$ python -m timeit 's = dict()'
5000000 loops, best of 5: 59.4 nsec per loop
```
which shouldn't be relevant in more than 99% cases, but still -- nice to be aware of it.

    def _members_search(...,
        scan=False,
        # add explicit scan_params
        scan_params=None,
        **kwargs
    ):
        """Members search."""
        ...
        # Prepare and execute the search
        params = params or {}
        # set explicit scan_params
        scan_params = scan_params or {}

        search = self._search(
            "search_members",
            identity,
            params,
            search_preference,
            search_opts=search_opts,
            permission_action=None,
            extra_filter=filter,
            **kwargs
        )

        # use explicit scan_params
        search_result = search.params(**scan_params).scan() if scan else search.execute()
```

@abtink Thanks for your explanation. I simply thought them as linear procedures - 3 parent requests then announce, and didn't get your intention of leveraging announce period and giving more chance to receive parent response by issuing the third parent request at the beginning of orphan announce. 

---

For the third phase, it seems to me not only `not wait long to collect more parent responses`, but also `not wait long enough to collect announce responses on all channels`. 

According to existing implementation, once the orphaned device receives an Announce Response from a different channel/pan, it will start a timer of 250ms `kAnnounceProcessTimeout` to collect announce responses (from devices working on the channels this orphan device has already announced on) or parent responses on the working channel, and **won't continue to send orphan announce on the remaining channels any more**. If there is no parent response during this period, the device will switch to the pan/channel for attach attempt. (side note - if the channel is different,  the unicast parent responses that are randomly delayed `< kParentResponseMaxDelayAll 1000ms` on original working channel will occupy the working channel for a while for retx due to NoAck)

I think it should wait orphan announce on all channels ends if there are no parent responses, then choose a channel/pan with **highest active timestamp** for attach attempts. Additionally,  it also makes sense to collect parent responses during whole announce period and accept **the best one available** after orphan announce ends.

I read some background on copyrights, and have come to a conclusion which I think is inline with most of your changes, but also supports the approach we're preferring. 

First, with respect to the Kolibri project as a whole, it is true that any 'material contribution' to the project should be individually copyrightable by the author making the contribution. If it isn't copyrightable by them, then that falls into the category of code that should clearly declare the copyright holder and the license that allows it as a contribution. So I think the meaningful aspect here is that distinction. 

Additionally, the project itself should be viewed as Joint Authorship, in that all contributors gain equal copyright on the resulting work, because the intent of most contributions is to create the single application, Kolibri. Therefore, tracking the individual copyright holder per file becomes unnecessary, because any originally authored contribution with that intent makes the contributor a joint author of the entire work. An originally authored contribution is and should be copyrightable, but as a contribution to a larger work, any presumed rights of that copyright aren't meaningful because joint authors of Kolibri also have equal rights. Anyone reusing bits of code from Kolibri should specify all joint authors because of this, by referencing the entire project's license and authorship information.

Furthermore, as an open source project, and a learning platform, we're definitely willing to recommend and advocate for best practices regarding code that is not originally authored by the contributor-- it should appropriately attribute its source and license. Framing it that way in our docs makes a lot of sense, since our vision is that 'all learners [including the many junior, external contributors we involve] develop their own agency, create positive transformation, and flourish', which ideally results in positive change in open-source software development in regards to these challenges.

Lastly, if a contribution is somewhat standalone, in that the intent doesn't directly develop the single work itself, then it is appropriate to document copyrights and licensing explicitly. This can be the case for many auxiliary Kolibri plugins that do not serve to complete it as a learning platform application, but only add-on to its functionality at run time. Kolibri plugins can be distributed in separate repositories, and under separate licensing, which should likely be the case for those introducing auxiliary functionality.

If the data being advertised is the same, there's no conflict.  However, when the data changes, you get a conflict until nobody is advertising the stale data anymore. So what we do is to just try to advertise the new data; if we get a conflict, we try again shortly. When the last peer advertising the old data gets the update, it will advertise the new data and not get a conflict. So the effect should be that the update is visible to the network almost immediately, and then the other servers catch up quickly (we do exponential backoff just in case, but the first retry is at 15 seconds).

Also, however, see also https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-tllq-tsr-00.html, which fully addresses the problem.

I think this should instead take *all* the query params passed to nbgitpuller, and a 'target directory' (see review comment https://github.com/jupyterhub/nbgitpuller/pull/194/files#diff-d4ed3eb90f2e3f1b059692aeefaa13a8c67db3016cc824df0573b60f05d154cbR6), and `yield` progress lines to be shown to the user. This is how the current code shows the output of running commands to the terminal in nbgitpuller (see  https://github.com/jupyterhub/nbgitpuller/blob/4d40c2d0445213e1b94a63e2940cccc841f1ced3/nbgitpuller/pull.py#L254), and is very helpful for the user to see what exactly is happening right now, and to copy error messages when things fail.

It'll also need to be *async* - otherwise the whole web server will 'block' while the download is happening. If you look at https://github.com/jupyterhub/nbgitpuller/blob/4d40c2d0445213e1b94a63e2940cccc841f1ced3/nbgitpuller/pull.py#L12, you can see that we call git asynchronously (with a `Popen` rather than `check_output`), and `yield` each line of output as progress. We also `yield` the name of the command we are executing so the user can see that too. This function should thus adopt this pattern as well, and become an async generator

Yes - I agree that we always want the same behavior when you depend on a `python_distribution`, and I think that behavior fairly obviously should be "build the dist and install it in the downstream environment".  Having a dependency mean "depend on the python_library targets that a python_distribution depends on", seems weird. And if someone really wants to depend on those python_library targets, they can just depend on them directly. 

In basically 100% of cases today, depending on a `python_distribution` is a smell, and we have masked the smell by giving that dependency a not-particularly-useful meaning. And I doubt there are many such dependencies today, since it doesn't really make sense, especially with dep inference. What I'm proposing is to have that dependency mean something more intuitive.

That said, again, dep inference really muddies the water...

Unfortunately, that is not quite true. As you can see [here](https://github.com/Sceptre/sceptre/blob/master/sceptre/cli/__init__.py#L76-L82) on lines 76-82, duplicate dictionary keys in the first `var_file` are deleted in favour of those appearing in the second `var_file`. This behaviour is further illustrated in the tests [here](https://github.com/Sceptre/sceptre/blob/master/tests/test_cli.py#L113-L125).

Moreover, the context object does not allow us to pass in the raw dictionaries from the `var_files`, but has only a single field [`user_variables`](https://github.com/Sceptre/sceptre/blob/master/sceptre/cli/__init__.py#L62), where it is expected that these dictionaries are already merged.

The `ConfigReader` class then initialises the `user_variables` [here](https://github.com/Sceptre/sceptre/blob/master/sceptre/config/reader.py#L132.

This is why I did not think my change here should refactor this code. 

I suppose, what we could do is remove the `user_variables` input to `ConfigReader`, and replace it with a list of `--var-files` and a list of `--vars`. Then, the code in the `cli` function could be moved to a private method of the `ConfigReader` class.

The idea was to have a simple type that managed what happened to the pointer after a move operation.
I thought it would make people's lives easier to control the state of their type-erased objects after move. For example a function like `is_empty()` could be defined as:
```
bool is_empty() const { return storage.is_empty(); }
``` 
But then, depending on the storage type, `is_empty()` might have different values after move. Indeed, using `storage_heap`, then after move, `is_empty() == false`, but if using `storage_stack`, then `is_empty() == true` after move (if the original object contained something).

So i thought, maybe if your custom function pointers were managed by `managed_move_pointer` and `is_empty()` was redefined as:
```
bool is_empty() const { return storage.is_empty() && func; }
```

then after a move operation, `is_empty() == false` always, regardless of storage type. It just ensures a bit more consistency across storage types.

But, i'm not sure where I stand here. In my opinion, if an object is valid, then it's valid, and you shouldn't make it artificially not valid. But that means you can't guarantee what the state after move will be, other than it's "valid". But that's precisely what move is supposed to be: "valid" but "undetermined".
So maybe we never need `managed_move_pointer`.

Then the idea of having `null_after_move=false` was to easily switch what moved did. If `null_after_move=false` then it's just the same as vanilla pointer.

So there you go, you get to configure what the state after move is a bit more.
